history.notes: Dopamine detox app riding the viral TikTok digital detox and sleepmaxxing trends; low keyword competition with growing search volume and clear subscription monetization.
Advancing to research — spawning Validator subagent

KILL: 10+ strong competitors (Opal at $10M ARR, One Sec with 22K ratings, Forest, plus 7+ dedicated dopamine detox apps) far exceed saturation threshold; directly competes with Apple's built-in Screen Time (a documented failure pattern); Apple has history of removing third-party screen time apps (2019 crackdown, Congressional antitrust hearings).

killReasons: Competitor saturation exceeds threshold (10+): Opal ($10M ARR, 4.8 stars, 200K downloads/mo), One Sec (4.83 stars, 22K ratings), Forest (established), Dopamine Detox: Restrict Apps, Elqi, FocusZone, Zario, Doxo AI, AppBlock, Cleverest, plus Apple Screen Time built-in. No clear gap — Opal already dominates with beautiful design and gamification.
Directly competes with Apple's built-in Screen Time feature — scout learnings explicitly flag this as a failure pattern ('Apps competing directly with Apple's built-in apps').

Apple has documented history of removing/restricting third-party screen time apps (2019 crackdown per CNN, 9to5Mac; Tim Cook questioned in 2020 Congressional antitrust hearing per TechCrunch). Category carries elevated App Store review risk.

demandSignals: TikTok 'dopamine detox' and 'digital detox 2026' content surging with hundreds of thousands of views
Multiple news outlets covering 'Dopamine Overload: Tech Detox in 2026' (ActionNews5, WNEM, WGEM, Jan 2026)
Google Trends 2026 wellness reports list digital detox as a key trend (chaindrugreview.com, ericadiamond.com)
marketSize.notes: Demand signals are real — the trend is growing. But demand alone is insufficient when the market is already saturated with well-funded competitors. The $1.12B global digital detox app market (per growthmarketreports.com) means incumbents are already capturing this demand.

competitors.identifiedGap: None that would justify entry. The scout's original hypothesis ('existing apps are too complex or lack engaging design') is invalidated — Opal specifically is praised for beautiful design and simplicity, and has $10M ARR proving product-market fit.
competitors.notes: Exceeds max_competitor_saturation threshold of 10. Additionally, Apple Screen Time is a built-in zero-cost competitor that ships on every iPhone.

monetization.mrrMath: In a saturated market, a new app might get 500-1000 downloads/mo. At 3% conversion to $3.99/mo = 15-30 subscribers = $60-$120 MRR. Even optimistic 2000 downloads/mo at 5% conversion = $400/mo. Does not reach medium threshold ($500+) within 3 months given competitor dominance.
monetization.notes: Opal captures the premium segment at $99.99/yr. Competing at $3.99/mo ($29.99/yr) leaves little room. Users who want free use Apple Screen Time. Users who want premium use Opal. There is no viable price point in between that generates meaningful MRR for a new entrant.

risks: App blocking requires Screen Time API (Family Controls framework) which has restrictions and requires special entitlements
Family Controls entitlement requires Apple approval and has been historically difficult to obtain
technicalFeasibility.notes: Basic challenge/streak/timer features are buildable in 1-2 weeks with SwiftUI. However, the core differentiating feature (app blocking) requires Family Controls entitlement which adds approval friction. Without app blocking, the app is just a timer with challenges — even less differentiated.

concerns: Apple removed/restricted multiple third-party screen time apps in 2019 (reported by CNN, 9to5Mac, NYT)
Apple CEO Tim Cook was questioned about this removal in 2020 Congressional antitrust hearing (TechCrunch)
EU antitrust complaints filed by Kidslox and Qustodio over Apple's anti-competitive behavior in this exact category
Family Controls entitlement required for app blocking features — Apple controls the approval gate
Category directly overlaps with Apple's built-in Screen Time feature
appStoreRisk.notes: While some screen time apps have survived (Opal, One Sec), they are well-funded companies that can fight back. A solo indie app in this category faces elevated review risk and Apple could restrict functionality at any iOS update.
